Catalog Description

Networks and Distributed Processing. (3-0). Credit 3. Basic hardware/software,

architectural components for computer communications; computer networks,

switching, routing, protocols and security; multiprocessing and distributed

processing; interfacing operating systems and networks; case studies of existing

networks and network architectures. Prerequisite: CSCE 313 or approval of

instructor.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  CSCE 313 or approval of instructor.

Course Learning Outcomes

At the end of the course students will be able to utilize operating-system sockets

APIs, create efficient network applications and build multi-threaded decentralized

frameworks, analyze performance and security of networks, develop protocols, and

construct models of their system.

Special Course Designation

None

Attendance and Active Classroom Participation

Coming prepared   for class is defined as having studied the material

covered in the previous class or any pre-assigned readings and arriving at

class on time.

10% of the course grade will be assigned to attendance, active classroom

participation and general classroom behavior and etiquette. Here are the

things that can result in points deducted

Missing more than 2 classes without prior notification and acceptable

absence (See attendance policy section)

Cell phone use

Arriving more than 5 minutes late to class

Having earbuds/headphones on (even if not being used)

Browsing Internet, playing games or working on other course work

during class time

Random walks in and out of class

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Each student gets  one free pass  for a late submission (up to 3 days) with

no penalty. The free pass must be claimed before the deadline (student

must email the professor). One day late submission will have 10% points

deducted, two days late submission will have 20% points deducted. No later

submissions will be graded. The free pass can be reclaimed for extra credit

at the end of the semester if never utilized (great deal!)

Students with 504 accommodations will get the necessary accommodations

per their accommodation letter

Additional Course Information

Course Assessments and activities:

Course Project

The course project will be completed in teams of 2-3 students

The project will aggregate different development technologies – at a course

that is a 400-level course – students are expected to utilize their

programming knowledge and ability to research resources to complete the

project requirements. This means a lot of self-learning will be required from

students. Time and dedication are essential and expected. Utilizing the right

resources in the correct way is encouraged – including generative AI tools.

that is a 400-level course – students are expected to utilize their

programming knowledge and ability to research resources to complete the

project requirements. This means a lot of self-learning will be required from

students. Time and dedication are essential and expected. Utilizing the right

resources in the correct way is encouraged – including generative AI tools.

Any code segments used from generative AI models must be thoroughly

verified (line by line), tested carefully and given proper citation.

Peer reviews will be part of the project assessment process, 30% of the final

project grade will be allocated to individual contribution.

Assignments

Assignments will be regularly posted to Canvas along with the due dates.

Submission of assignments must be done through Canvas by the assigned

due date – emailed submissions shall not be graded!

Each assignment will have specific submission requirements; students are

expected to read and understand each of these requirements and ask

questions if in doubt.

Assignments will alternate between coding assignments, hands-on

Wireshark lab assignments, and CISCO packet tracer labs.

The professor reserves the right to discuss the submitted work with any

student if there is any suspicion or evidence of online or peer copying

without proper citations.

Exams

All exams will assess the students’ understanding of the concepts discussed

throughout the course as well as their ability to apply them correctly to

solve different problems.

Students are expected to complete on their own some pre-identified end of

chapter problems from the textbook, similar problems should be expected

in exams.

Teaching Strategies

Among human innovations, computers and networking stand out as having

the greatest impact on society: think of our world before and after computers

and the Internet. In this course we will learn and understand that networking

in its essence is the science of orchestrating layered protocols to enable

seamless communication.

Networking is also the science of balancing competing goals: efficiency,

reliability, fairness, scalability, and security, where every gain comes with a

cost elsewhere. These ideas bring in rich discussions and conversations in the

classroom, as computer scientists, our mission is to question the design of

protocols, identify trade-offs and think innovatively to solve some of the

pressing problems in networking.

The course will provide a platform for rich discussions and analysis of the

cost elsewhere. These ideas bring in rich discussions and conversations in the

classroom, as computer scientists, our mission is to question the design of

protocols, identify trade-offs and think innovatively to solve some of the

pressing problems in networking.

The course will provide a platform for rich discussions and analysis of the

different network layers in a top-down approach. Students are encouraged to

actively participate in these discussion.

Understanding the Course Academic Integrity

In this course, all assignments should reflect your own work unless it’s a

group assignment. You are allowed to discuss high-level concepts with your

classmates, but you are not allowed to share code nor look at each other’s

code or solutions. Academic integrity is crucial and will be taken seriously, as

it upholds the principles of honesty, fairness, and originality in students' work.

For team projects, each student's active contribution to their team is essential

for the successful completion of the project, as it upholds the integrity and

collaborative spirit expected in this course. By contributing to the project and

not relying on teammates doing all the work will foster a sense of

responsibility and accountability, which are key values in both academic and

professional settings. Teamwork encourages mutual respect, trust, and

communication, qualities that are vital for success in the real world.

AI Statement

Unless indicated in the assignment or project and authorized by the professor, the

use of generative AI to complete the work will be considered a violation of academic

integrity. If in doubt about what it means or counts as a utilization of generative AI,

ask the professor prior to attempting the task to avoid any unexpected penalties, in

other words: Ask for permission instead of forgiveness.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

If for any reason you are unable to attend a class you will need to email the

professor for an excused absence. The professor reserves the rights to assess if the

reason is valid or not and request any supporting documentation.

Missing quizzes or exams does not guarantee a makeup unless the reason involves

significant and unavoidable circumstances, such as severe illness or

emergencies, and is properly documented .
